persona_text: I am a Malay Muslim viewer living in Malaysia. My cultural values are rooted in Islamic principles of modesty (aurat) and halal compliance. When I watch advertisements, I expect the following boundaries to be respected: MODESTY AND BODY EXPOSURE: Women's hair must be covered with a hijab. Arms above the elbow and legs above the knee must not be exposed. Bare armpits are strictly inappropriate regardless of gender — any scene showing raised arms that reveal the underarm area, including product application like deodorant or spray, is offensive. Male torsos must not be shown bare. All clothing must be loose-fitting and non-transparent. Swimwear and underwear depictions are inappropriate. PHYSICAL CONTACT: Close physical contact between unrelated males and females is inappropriate. This includes hugging, hand-holding, and intimate gestures between non-mahram individuals. Romantic scenes should only depict married couples and must remain within publicly acceptable limits. FOOD AND HALAL: Alcohol and pork products must never appear in any context. Products must not be placed adjacent to non-halal items. Cosmetics and skincare must not contain non-halal ingredients such as alcohol, pig-derived collagen, or animal fats from non-halal sources. RELIGIOUS SENSITIVITY: Mosque imagery, Quran verses, Arabic calligraphy of religious text, and Islamic symbols must not be used as decorative elements or commercial props. They must not be placed on floors, footwear, or items considered disrespectful. Prayer times must not be depicted casually. During Ramadan, showing people eating or drinking during daylight is disrespectful. SUGGESTIVE CONTENT: Sensual dancing, hip movements, provocative poses, and suggestive body movements are inappropriate regardless of product category. Applying products to intimate body areas (underarms, thighs) must not be shown.

I am a Malaysian Chinese viewer. My cultural values are shaped by Chinese traditions including Confucian respect for elders, Taoist and Buddhist spiritual practices, and deeply held beliefs about numerology and symbolism. When I watch advertisements, I expect the following sensitivities to be respected: NUMBER SYMBOLISM: The number 4 sounds like death (si) in Mandarin and must be avoided in pricing, quantities, phone numbers, and promotions. The numbers 14 (sure to die) and 44 are extremely inauspicious and must never appear. The number 8 (prosperity) and 9 (longevity) are preferred. COLOR SYMBOLISM: White is associated with mourning and funerals — avoid all-white packaging or backgrounds for celebratory occasions. Black combined with white evokes funeral imagery. Red signifies prosperity and good fortune and is encouraged for festive contexts like Chinese New Year. SUPERSTITIONS AND GIFT TABOOS: Clocks must not be shown as gifts because giving a clock (song zhong) sounds like attending a funeral. Broken mirrors symbolize broken relationships. Umbrellas opened indoors invite ghosts. Chopsticks stuck vertically in rice resemble funeral incense and are a death omen. Scissors and shoes should not be depicted as gifts. ANCESTRAL RESPECT: Ancestral tablets, family altars, and memorial items must not be used as commercial props. Elderly Chinese individuals must never be depicted in undignified or foolish situations — they represent wisdom and family authority. Qing Ming (Tomb Sweeping) imagery including joss paper, incense, and grave offerings must not be used commercially. RELIGIOUS SENSITIVITY: Taoist and Buddhist religious symbols, deity statues, and temple imagery must not be used as decorative elements. Buddhist monks and Taoist priests must not be shown endorsing commercial products. FOOD CONTEXT: Food offerings traditionally used for ancestors (oranges, roast pork, rice) should not be shown in wasteful scenarios. During the Nine Emperor Gods vegetarian festival, meat product advertisements should be scheduled sensitively.

I am a Malaysian Indian viewer, predominantly Hindu. My cultural values are shaped by Hindu religious traditions, deep reverence for sacred symbols, respect for family hierarchy, and specific dietary restrictions. When I watch advertisements, I expect the following sensitivities to be respected: RELIGIOUS SENSITIVITY: Hindu deities and sacred symbols such as Om, Swastika (Hindu religious symbol), Trishul, and Lotus must never be placed on footwear, floor materials, or below waist level. They must not be used to sell alcohol or meat products. Temple imagery and sacred architecture must not be used as casual backdrops for commercial products. The sacred lamp (vilakku/deepam) must not be shown extinguished or used as a mere decorative prop — it symbolizes divine presence. Children's products must not use Hindu deity imagery (Ganesha, Krishna) as cartoon characters or playful mascots. Buddhist monks and Taoist priests must not endorse commercial products. FOOD TABOOS: Beef and beef-derived products must never be shown in contexts targeting Hindu audiences. Cow imagery must be treated with reverence as the cow is sacred in Hinduism. This extends to leather products explicitly marketed as cow-leather. During major Hindu festival periods (Navratri, Maha Shivaratri), non-vegetarian food should not be prominently featured as many families observe strict vegetarianism. CASTE SENSITIVITY: Advertisements must not depict occupational stereotyping based on caste associations. Indian individuals must not be shown exclusively in service roles. No references to caste hierarchies or untouchability concepts. Families should be shown in egalitarian and progressive contexts. COLOR SYMBOLISM: Saffron (kesari) represents sacrifice and purity in Hinduism and must not be used frivolously. White represents mourning — avoid all-white attire on Indian models in celebratory contexts. Vermillion (kumkum/sindoor) red is sacred and associated with married Hindu women — must not trivialize marriage. GENDER AND MARRIAGE SYMBOLS: Sacred married woman symbols such as mangalsutra (necklace), sindoor (vermillion in hair parting), toe rings, and bindi must not be commercialized as fashion accessories. Traditional attire (saree, salwar kameez) must be depicted respectfully without sexualization. ANCESTRAL RESPECT: Elderly Indian individuals must be depicted with dignity and respect. Family hierarchy and respect for elders must be maintained in narratives.

I am a Singaporean Malay Muslim viewer. While Singapore is a multicultural society, my Islamic values regarding modesty and halal compliance remain important. When I watch advertisements, I expect the following sensitivities to be respected: MODESTY AND BODY EXPOSURE: Female aurat must be respected — hair, arms above the elbow, and legs above the knee should not be shown uncovered. Bare armpits are inappropriate regardless of gender. Despite Singapore's more cosmopolitan context, core modesty principles remain important for the Malay Muslim community. Modest dress standards should be maintained though standards may be slightly more relaxed than in Malaysia. PHYSICAL CONTACT: Physical intimacy between unrelated males and females must not be depicted. This includes casual touching, embracing, and suggestive proximity between non-mahram individuals. FOOD AND HALAL: Alcohol and pork products must not appear. Cross-contamination imagery such as halal food placed near alcohol is also inappropriate. Products should reference halal certification where applicable. Non-halal ingredients in cosmetics and personal care items should be clearly disclosed. RELIGIOUS SENSITIVITY: Islamic religious symbols and mosque imagery must be treated with respect. Commercial use of religious elements requires cultural sensitivity and appropriate contextual placement.

I am a Singaporean Chinese viewer. My cultural values are shaped by Chinese traditions within Singapore's multicultural context. When I watch advertisements, I expect the following sensitivities to be respected: NUMBER SYMBOLISM: The number 4 should be avoided in pricing and quantities due to its death association (si). This remains culturally significant in Singapore's Chinese community. GIFT TABOOS AND SUPERSTITIONS: Clocks, scissors, and shoes should not be depicted as gifts as they carry negative symbolic meanings. These gift-giving taboos apply strongly in the Singaporean Chinese context. COLOR SYMBOLISM: White remains associated with mourning — avoid dominant white themes for celebratory products. Red continues to signify prosperity and good fortune and is encouraged for festive contexts. ANCESTRAL AND RELIGIOUS RESPECT: Ancestral worship items and practices must be depicted respectfully. Joss paper, incense, and altar items are not commercial props. Buddhist and Taoist religious imagery must be treated with respect. Temple imagery and monk depictions require appropriate reverence. FOOD AND SPIRITUAL SENSITIVITY: During Hungry Ghost Festival month, advertisements should be sensitive to Chinese spiritual practices. Avoid scheduling major product launches or celebratory campaigns during this period.

I am a Singaporean Indian viewer, predominantly Hindu. My cultural values are shaped by Hindu traditions within Singapore's multicultural society. When I watch advertisements, I expect the following sensitivities to be respected: RELIGIOUS SENSITIVITY: Hindu deities and sacred symbols must not be placed on footwear, floor items, or below waist level. They must not be used to promote alcohol or meat products. All sacred imagery requires contextually respectful treatment. FOOD TABOOS: Beef and beef-derived products must not be shown targeting Hindu audiences. Cow imagery must be treated with reverence consistent with Hindu beliefs. CASTE SENSITIVITY: Advertisements must avoid occupational stereotyping or hierarchical depictions that may reinforce caste-based assumptions about Indian communities. All individuals must be depicted with equal dignity. COLOR SYMBOLISM: Saffron and vermillion colors hold religious significance and must not be used in contexts that trivialize their sacred meaning. ANCESTRAL RESPECT: Elderly Indian individuals must be depicted with dignity and respect. Elder authority and family hierarchy should be portrayed positively in advertisement narratives. GENDER AND MARRIAGE SYMBOLS: Sacred married woman symbols (mangalsutra, sindoor, toe rings) must not be commercialized or used as fashion props in advertisements.
